hypothesis: A recurrent neural network (RNN) trained on patient medication adherence time series data predicts hospital readmission rates better than a traditional logistic regression model using baseline demographics.
domain: Healthcare/clinical ML using electronic health records (EHR)
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy
The experiment uses 36 months of EHR records from a single healthcare provider system, comprising approximately 100,000 patient records. The data includes timestamped medication adherence logs (daily or weekly aggregates), discharge dates, 30-day readmission outcomes, and baseline patient attributes (age, sex, insurance, comorbidity scores, discharge department). The outcome label is binary: readmission within 30 days of discharge. Patients lacking 30-day follow-up data are excluded to avoid censoring. All features and labels are collected prospectively and are available at the time of discharge or shortly thereafter; no retrospective imputation of missing outcomes is performed.

### 2. Randomized and Stratified Split Strategy
We utilize stratified random sampling with proportionate representation of discharge departments to divide the dataset. A random 60% of patients is drawn for training, 20% for validation, and 20% for testing. Stratification ensures that discharge department representation remains consistent across train, validation, and test sets, effectively accounting for provider practice variability.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Two feature sets are constructed, one for each model. For the RNN, the primary input is the medication adherence time series for the 180 days preceding discharge, represented as a sequence of adherence rates (e.g., weekly averages of the proportion of doses taken on schedule). The sequence is padded or truncated to a uniform length of 180 timesteps. For logistic regression, features include: baseline demographics (age, sex, insurance type), Charlson comorbidity score, discharge department, and summary statistics of adherence (mean and standard deviation over the pre-discharge period). To ensure stability in preprocessing, all transformers are fit on the entire dataset before any splits. This approach mitigates the risk of parameter instability due to small training sample sizes, leading to consistent preprocessing across train, validation, and test sets.

### 4. Model and Baseline
The RNN model is a bidirectional LSTM with 64 hidden units, one recurrent layer, dropout (0.3), and a sigmoid output layer producing a probability of 30-day readmission. It is trained with binary cross-entropy loss and Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-3). The logistic regression baseline uses the same training data and baseline feature set (demographics and adherence summary statistics), allowing a fair comparison: both models are trained on the same 60% of patients (drawing from stratified samples) and given equivalent tuning effort (hyperparameter search on validation data). The baseline tests the null hypothesis that a simple, non-temporal model is sufficient; the RNN is superior only if it materially improves performance by exploiting the sequential structure of adherence.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The primary metric is area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). Readmission is a low-prevalence outcome, and the clinical objective is to identify at-risk patients for timely intervention. AUPRC directly reflects the trade-off between precision (reducing false alarms) and recall (minimizing missed cases) and is insensitive to the majority class, making it more appropriate than AUROC for imbalanced settings. Secondary metrics include AUROC (for threshold-independent comparison), F1 score at the optimal validation threshold, sensitivity at 90% specificity (to characterize false-positive rate at a fixed operating point), and calibration metrics (expected calibration error, calibration plots) to ensure predicted probabilities are clinically actionable.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection
Hyperparameters for both the RNN (hidden unit count, dropout, learning rate) and logistic regression (L2 regularization) are selected via grid or random search, evaluated on the validation set using AUPRC as the selection criterion. The test set is held in reserve and is not accessed until after model selection is finalized. Final performance estimates, including 95% confidence intervals, are computed on the test set using all evaluation metrics.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope
Provider variability is controlled via departmental stratification and by including department as a feature in both models. Post-hoc stratified analysis on the test set (separate AUPRC by department) will detect any systematic performance variation by provider. Temporal trends are naturally accounted for by the randomized sampling strategy. Patient socioeconomic factors are partially controlled via insurance type as a proxy. Class imbalance is not corrected via resampling (to maintain realistic prevalence) but is addressed through metric choice (AUPRC) and secondary metrics. The experiment establishes whether the RNN outperforms logistic regression on this specific provider's data over the 36-month window; generalization to other providers, healthcare systems, or populations is out of scope and would require external validation.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split instead of a temporal split, which risks training the model on data from after the test period. Since adherence and readmission outcomes are time-dependent, this allows future information to influence model training, resulting in over-optimistic performance estimates that do not reflect real-world deployment where predictions must be made without future data.

Preprocessing transformers (e.g., normalization, imputation) are fit on the entire dataset before train/validation/test splits, meaning statistics from validation and test sets are used during training. This leaks future and held-out information into the training pipeline, biasing the evaluation and violating the principle that preprocessing should only use training data to simulate real deployment conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of patients without 30-day follow-up introduces selection bias that invalidates the study.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding patients without 30-day follow-up is necessary to avoid censoring bias in readmission labeling. This is standard practice in clinical outcome modeling as the ground truth cannot be reliably established for these patients, and including them would introduce uncertainty rather than reduce bias.

must_not_claim.claim: Using AUPRC instead of AUROC is inappropriate because it doesn't reflect overall model performance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUPRC is the correct primary metric for highly imbalanced clinical prediction tasks like readmission, where false positives directly impact resource allocation. It focuses on the high-precision, low-recall regime that matters for targeted interventions, making it more operationally relevant than AUROC.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies either or both flaws related to temporal leakage or preprocessing leakage, explaining how these invalidate out-of-time generalization and introduce data leakage respectively. The debate concludes that both flaws must be corrected for a valid experiment: implement a temporal split and recompute preprocessing parameters solely on the training set.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that random sampling violates time-ordering and that fit-on-full-data preprocessing allows information from future or unseen samples to influence training, both of which would inflate performance in an unrealistic way.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: N/A — both corruptions represent high-severity methodological violations with no legitimate justification in this longitudinal clinical context.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly argues that despite the soundness of other design elements (e.g., choice of metric, baseline comparability), the presence of data leakage in splitting and preprocessing fundamentally undermines the validity of any performance comparison, rendering the conclusion unreliable even if the RNN appears to outperform the baseline.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer emphasizes that methodological rigor takes precedence over apparent results — a flawed evaluation process invalidates any claimed performance gain regardless of magnitude.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy
The experiment uses 36 months of EHR records from a single healthcare provider system, comprising approximately 100,000 patient records. The data includes timestamped medication adherence logs (daily or weekly aggregates), discharge dates, 30-day readmission outcomes, and baseline patient attributes (age, sex, insurance, comorbidity scores, discharge department). The outcome label is binary: readmission within 30 days of discharge. Patients lacking 30-day follow-up data are excluded to avoid censoring. All features and labels are collected prospectively and are available at the time of discharge or shortly thereafter; no retrospective imputation of missing outcomes is performed.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split Strategy
To prevent data leakage and account for provider-level confounding, the cohort is divided chronologically and departmentally. The timeline is split as follows: months 1–24 (training, 60%), months 25–30 (validation, 20%), months 31–36 (test, 20%). Within each temporal fold, patients are stratified by discharge department (e.g., cardiology, orthopedics, general medicine) to ensure proportional representation. This temporal split ensures that adherence data precedes discharge and readmission outcomes, eliminating future information leakage. Departmental stratification addresses variability in provider practices and readmission protocols across clinical units.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Two feature sets are constructed, one for each model. For the RNN, the primary input is the medication adherence time series for the 180 days preceding discharge, represented as a sequence of adherence rates (e.g., weekly averages of the proportion of doses taken on schedule). The sequence is padded or truncated to a uniform length of 180 timesteps. For logistic regression, features include: baseline demographics (age, sex, insurance type), Charlson comorbidity score, discharge department, and summary statistics of adherence (mean and standard deviation over the pre-discharge period). All preprocessing—z-score normalization of adherence, forward-fill then mean imputation of missing values, and categorical encoding—is fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents any information from validation or test leaking into the preprocessing parameters.

### 4. Model and Baseline
The RNN model is a bidirectional LSTM with 64 hidden units, one recurrent layer, dropout (0.3), and a sigmoid output layer producing a probability of 30-day readmission. It is trained with binary cross-entropy loss and Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-3). The logistic regression baseline uses the same training data and baseline feature set (demographics and adherence summary statistics), allowing a fair comparison: both models are trained on the same 60% of patients (months 1–24) and given equivalent tuning effort (hyperparameter search on validation data). The baseline tests the null hypothesis that a simple, non-temporal model is sufficient; the RNN is superior only if it materially improves performance by exploiting the sequential structure of adherence.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The primary metric is area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). Readmission is a low-prevalence outcome, and the clinical objective is to identify at-risk patients for timely intervention. AUPRC directly reflects the trade-off between precision (reducing false alarms) and recall (minimizing missed cases) and is insensitive to the majority class, making it more appropriate than AUROC for imbalanced settings. Secondary metrics include AUROC (for threshold-independent comparison), F1 score at the optimal validation threshold, sensitivity at 90% specificity (to characterize false-positive rate at a fixed operating point), and calibration metrics (expected calibration error, calibration plots) to ensure predicted probabilities are clinically actionable.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection
Hyperparameters for both the RNN (hidden unit count, dropout, learning rate) and logistic regression (L2 regularization) are selected via grid or random search, evaluated on the validation set (months 25–30) using AUPRC as the selection criterion. The test set (months 31–36) is held in reserve and is not accessed until after model selection is finalized. Final performance estimates, including 95% confidence intervals, are computed on the test set using all evaluation metrics.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope
Provider variability is controlled via departmental stratification and by including department as a feature in both models. Post-hoc stratified analysis on the test set (separate AUPRC by department) will detect any systematic performance variation by provider. Temporal trends are naturally accounted for by the chronological split. Patient socioeconomic factors are partially controlled via insurance type as a proxy. Class imbalance is not corrected via resampling (to maintain realistic prevalence) but is addressed through metric choice (AUPRC) and secondary metrics. The experiment establishes whether the RNN outperforms logistic regression on this specific provider's data over the 36-month window; generalization to other providers, healthcare systems, or populations is out of scope and would require external validation.
difficulty_justification: The case is moderately difficult because the primary flaw (temporal leakage) is subtle and masked by correct-sounding language around 'stratified sampling,' while the preprocessing leakage is more detectable but may be rationalized by concerns about data stability. A competent reviewer must integrate temporal reasoning with data leakage principles to fully critique the design.
